// SLIDE 01 — HOOK

CHAPTER 3 — CREATING AND WORKING IN DOCUMENTS

Start with a simple observation: a document that is twenty pages long is not the same problem as a document that is two

Chapter 3 — Creating and Working in Docu — The Navigation pane is the solution to this problem. In Word, you open it from the View tab. A sidebar appears on the le
NARRATION

Start with a simple observation: a document that is twenty pages long is not the same problem as a document that is two pages long. At two pages, you can see the whole thing. Your eye moves down the screen and you find what you need. At twenty pages, you are lost. You are somewhere on page eleven, you need to jump to the compliance section, and the only way to do it is to scroll , past everything you do not need, hunting for the thing you do.

// SLIDE 02 — THE DIFFERENCE BETWEEN APPEARA

THE DIFFERENCE BETWEEN APPEARANCE AND STRUCTURE

—
Setup — Setup — option applied to the chapter example.
Execution — Execution — option applied to the chapter example.
Verification — Verification — option applied to the chapter example.
NARRATION

Now we can say something more precise about what Word is actually doing. When you look at a document, you see text that appears in different ways: some text is bigger, some is bold, some is in a serif font, some in a sans-serif font. That is appearance. Separately, underneath the appearance, the document has structure: this is a heading, this is body text, this is a caption. Structure is about what a piece of text is, not how it looks.

// SLIDE 03 — HOW COLLABORATION WORKS WITHOU

HOW COLLABORATION WORKS WITHOUT CHAOS.

—
Setup — Use when setup is the main constraint.
Execution — Use when execution is the main constraint.
Verification — Use when verification is the main constraint.
NARRATION

So far everything has been about one person working on a document. That is already enough to get lost in. But most workplace documents are not solo. They go back and forth. A draft goes to a manager, comes back with edits, goes to a client, comes back with questions, goes to legal, comes back with revisions. The question is: how do you track what changed? The naive approach is to have each person save a new version. "Draft_v1.docx," "Draft_v2_Partner_Comments.docx," "Draft_v3_Final.docx," "Draft_v3_Final_REALLY.docx." This approach fails reliably.

// SLIDE 04 — WHAT A TEMPLATE ACTUALLY IS

WHAT A TEMPLATE ACTUALLY IS.

The three concepts — navigation via structured headings, formatting via styles, collaboration via Track Changes and Comm

What a Template Actually Is — A template is a document that has already been set up. The headings are structured. The styles are defined. The margins
NARRATION

The three concepts , navigation via structured headings, formatting via styles, collaboration via Track Changes and Comments , come together in the template. A template is a document that has already been set up. The headings are structured. The styles are defined. The margins and fonts and spacing are set. When you open a template and begin editing, you are editing inside a framework that someone built in advance. You do not have to create the structure; it is already there.

// SLIDE 05 — PUTTING IT TOGETHER

PUTTING IT TOGETHER.

The Navigation pane, styles, and Track Changes are not three unrelated features you have to memorize. They are three sol

Putting It Together — The Navigation pane makes a document navigable. A reader who needs section four does not have to scroll through sections
NARRATION

The Navigation pane, styles, and Track Changes are not three unrelated features you have to memorize. They are three solutions to the same underlying problem: a document has to be usable by someone other than the person who wrote it. The Navigation pane makes a document navigable. A reader who needs section four does not have to scroll through sections one through three. They click. They are there.

// SLIDE 06 — CHAPTER SUMMARY

CHAPTER SUMMARY

A document's tools exist to solve real problems. The Navigation pane exists because long documents are impossible to nav

Chapter Summary — Templates combine all three: they are documents where someone has already set up the structure, defined the styles, and
NARRATION

A document's tools exist to solve real problems. The Navigation pane exists because long documents are impossible to navigate without structure , but structure only exists in the Navigation pane if you have marked headings with heading styles, not just manually formatted text to look like headings. Styles exist because appearance and structure are separate things, and decoupling them makes global formatting changes take seconds instead of hours.

// SLIDE 07 — EXERCISES

EXERCISES

3.1→3.2→3.3→3.4→3.5
NARRATION

3.1 (The promise a document makes.) Name three differences between a text message and a professional document , not in how they look, but in what they are expected to do for the reader. For each difference, identify which tool from this chapter (Navigation pane, styles, or Track Changes) is most directly responsible for making that capability possible. 3.2 (Appearance vs. structure.) You open a Word document someone sent you. The section titles are large, bold, and in a different font from the body text.

// SLIDE 08 — AI WAYBACK MACHINE

AI WAYBACK MACHINE.

Ask it to explain Simonyi's distinction between "meta-programming" and "programming"and how that distinction shows up in modern Word's styles and templates
Ask"Simonyi was born in Hungary in 1948 and learned to program on a Soviet-made Ural-2 computer as a teenager. How did that
Add the constraint"Answer using one specific Word feature most users don't realize is there but that Simonyi designed deliberately"
NARRATION

The ideas in this chapter didn't appear from nowhere. Charles Simonyi was working on Bravo at Xerox PARC, the first WYSIWYG word processor , then Microsoft Word, which he led for the company's first two decades decades before most people had heard of word processing. Here's a prompt to find out more , and then make it better.

// SLIDE 09 — PROMPTS

PROMPTS

Create a single-file D3 v7 HTML comparison using 4-6 labeled nodes or panels, directional connectors where sequence matt

Prompts — Create a single-file D3 v7 HTML conceptual map using 4-6 labeled nodes or panels, directional connectors where sequence
NARRATION

Create a single-file D3 v7 HTML comparison using 4-6 labeled nodes or panels, directional connectors where sequence matters, direct labels on every mark, one primary red emphasis mark, neutral supporting marks, and no unlabeled boxes. The figure should represent: Word document split-view showing Navigation pane on the left with Headings tab active, and the document body on the right , the heading "Compliance" is highlighted in both the pane and the document body, illustrating the click-to-jump relationship.

// SLIDE 10 — THESIS

CHAPTER SUMMARY

A document's tools exist to solve real problems. The Navigation pane exists because long documents are impossible to nav

Templates combine all three: they are documents where someone has already set up the structure, defined the styles, and

NARRATION

A document's tools exist to solve real problems. The Navigation pane exists because long documents are impossible to navigate without structure , but structure only exists in the Navigation pane if you have marked headings with heading styles, not just manually formatted text to look like headings. Styles exist because appearance and structure are separate things, and decoupling them makes global formatting changes take seconds instead of hours.
